heroOverview: Streptobacillosis is a bacterial infection caused by Streptobacillus moniliformis, the dominant pathogen responsible for rat-bite fever in North America and Europe. It accounts for roughly 200 recognised cases annually in the United States alone, though experts believe the true incidence is substantially higher because many cases are misdiagnosed or never reported. The organism is found in the nasopharyngeal secretions of 50–100% of wild and domestic rats that appear entirely healthy. Transmission occurs through a rat bite, scratch, or occasionally ingestion of rat-contaminated food or water — the latter producing an outbreak form known as Haverhill fever. Untreated streptobacillosis carries a case-fatality rate of approximately 10%, largely from endocarditis; a 10–14 day course of penicillin is curative in the great majority of patients.

keyStats.prevalence: Estimated 200 reported U.S. cases annually; true incidence is substantially higher due to underdiagnosis; dominant form of rat-bite fever in North America and Europe
keyStats.demographics: Laboratory animal workers, pet-rat owners, children in rodent-infested housing, and people in crowded urban environments are most affected
keyStats.avgAge: All ages affected; children under 12 and young adults with pet rats represent the most commonly reported groups in recent U.S. series
keyStats.globalCases: Global burden is not precisely known; reported worldwide but most frequently in the United States, United Kingdom, and Western Europe

definition: Streptobacillosis (ICD-10: A25.1) is a systemic bacterial zoonosis caused by Streptobacillus moniliformis, a gram-negative, pleomorphic, non-motile rod-shaped organism that forms chains and characteristic L-form cell-wall-deficient variants. Unlike the causative agent of spirillosis, Streptobacillus moniliformis can be cultured on enriched media such as blood agar supplemented with 10–20% serum, or in BACTEC blood culture systems with extended incubation, though it grows slowly and can be missed. The organism colonises the upper respiratory tract of rats asymptomatically; human infection follows inoculation through a bite or scratch, or in the Haverhill fever form, ingestion of contaminated water or unpasteurised milk. After a short incubation of 1–22 days (most often 3–10 days), streptobacillosis typically presents as an abrupt febrile illness accompanied by a characteristic migratory polyarthritis affecting the large joints in 50–70% of patients, and a maculopapular rash that is especially prominent on the extremities. In contrast to spirillosis, the bite wound usually heals normally and does not re-ulcerate, regional lymphadenopathy is mild, and arthritis is a dominant feature. The infection falls under infectious disease, though its arthritis may initially prompt rheumatological assessment.

typesClassification.description: The most common form; follows a rat bite or scratch, producing abrupt fever, migratory polyarthritis in 50–70% of patients, and a maculopapular rash on the palms, soles, and extremities after a 3–10 day incubation.

Caused by the same organism but acquired through ingestion of water, food, or unpasteurised milk contaminated with rat excreta; outbreaks involving multiple people from a single contaminated source have been recorded. Nausea and vomiting are more prominent, and the arthritis is usually less severe.

A rare but life-threatening form in which Streptobacillus moniliformis infects heart valves, either as a complication of untreated bacteraemia or in patients with pre-existing valve disease. It accounts for most streptobacillosis fatalities.

primarySymptoms: Abrupt onset of fever rising to 38–40°C, usually appearing 3–10 days after the rat bite and representing the start of bacteraemic dissemination from the wound site.

Migratory polyarthritis affecting large joints — knees, ankles, elbows, and wrists — occurring in 50–70% of patients; joints become swollen, tender, warm, and transiently immobile before migrating to another joint, sometimes resembling rheumatoid or reactive arthritis.
A maculopapular or petechial rash appearing on the trunk and extremities, with a predilection for the palms and soles, which develops 2–4 days after the onset of fever.
Severe headache, often retro-orbital or frontal, accompanying the febrile phase and subsiding as fever is treated.
Rigors and chills at the start of febrile episodes, though the fever pattern in streptobacillosis is typically sustained rather than the relapsing every-few-days pattern of spirillosis.
Nausea and vomiting during the febrile phase, particularly prominent in Haverhill fever where the gastrointestinal route of entry means more intense early GI symptoms.
Myalgia — diffuse muscle aching through the limbs and back — often overshadowed by the arthritis but independently present from early in the illness.
Malaise and fatigue that persist between febrile peaks and can last weeks in untreated patients.
Lymphadenopathy near the bite wound, though usually milder and less tender than the lymph node swelling seen in spirillosis or cat-scratch disease.
Throat pain and oral ulceration in Haverhill fever cases, reflecting initial mucosal infection from ingested organisms.
earlyWarningSigns: Fever starting 3–10 days after a rat bite even if the bite wound looked minor and has healed normally.
Swollen or painful joints, particularly multiple joints, appearing alongside or shortly after fever onset.
A fine maculopapular rash appearing on the hands, feet, or trunk a few days into an unexplained fever.
Multiple people developing similar febrile illness after sharing a potentially rodent-contaminated food or water source (Haverhill fever pattern).
emergencySigns: Chest pain, breathlessness, or a new cardiac murmur, which may indicate endocarditis — the most lethal complication.
Confusion, severe headache, or neck stiffness suggesting meningitis or severe sepsis.
High fever with low blood pressure, rapid pulse, or difficulty breathing consistent with septic shock.
Fever in an immunocompromised person following any rat contact, which warrants same-day evaluation.
Arthritis affecting multiple joints with fever persisting beyond 72 hours without diagnosis — septic arthritis must be excluded urgently.

causes.description: Streptobacillus moniliformis colonises the nasopharynx of 50–100% of healthy wild and domestic rats. A bite transfers the organism into the dermis, where it enters lymphatics and bloodstream after a short incubation of 3–10 days. Even minor scratches from rat claws, which can carry oral secretions from grooming, are an effective transmission route.

Veterinary workers, pet shop employees, and laboratory animal technicians can acquire infection from rat saliva on minor cuts or mucous-membrane exposure without a frank bite; rat handling without gloves is the most common occupational mechanism.

causes.cause: Ingestion of contaminated food or water (Haverhill fever)
causes.description: Streptobacillus moniliformis shed in rat urine and faeces can contaminate water supplies, unpasteurised milk, and food in rat-infested buildings; ingestion produces a form of the illness known as Haverhill fever, which can affect multiple people from a common source and may occur without any direct rat contact.

causes.cause: Contact with rodents other than rats
causes.description: Mice, gerbils, squirrels, and other rodents can carry S. moniliformis and occasionally transmit the organism through bites or scratches, though rats are by far the most common source in reported human cases.

Cats and ferrets that prey on infected rodents can harbour S. moniliformis in their oral secretions and transfer it via bites or scratches, accounting for a small subset of cases in households where both pet rats and predators are kept.

riskFactors.description: The single largest risk factor in contemporary U.S. and European case series; domestic rats from pet shops, breeders, and rescues can carry S. moniliformis asymptomatically. Children are particularly represented because unsupervised handling is more frequent and bites during play are common.

riskFactors.factor: Laboratory and veterinary work with rodents
riskFactors.description: Animal research technicians, vivarium staff, and veterinarians who handle rodents daily have cumulative exposure risk; mandatory bite-reporting, gloves, and safety protocols reduce but do not eliminate risk in this setting.

Poverty, crowded urban housing, and inadequate rodent pest control increase the frequency of incidental rat bites, particularly in sleeping children; community cases disproportionately involve children under 12 from lower-income urban households.

HIV infection, organ transplantation, chemotherapy, and high-dose corticosteroids impair bacterial clearance and predispose to severe, disseminated streptobacillosis with a higher risk of endocarditis, septic arthritis, and meningitis.

Patients with rheumatic, congenital, or degenerative valve disease are at elevated risk for Streptobacillus endocarditis if bacteraemia is prolonged; antibiotic prophylaxis after a bite may be appropriate in this group.

riskFactors.factor: Ingestion of potentially contaminated water or food
riskFactors.description: In settings with known rodent infestations, consuming unpasteurised milk or unboiled water carries a risk of Haverhill fever; basic food safety measures effectively eliminate this route.

affectedDemographics: Children under 12 with pet rats, the most common group in contemporary U.S. case reports
Young adults aged 18–35 who own pet rats or work in pet shops or rodent laboratories
Laboratory animal care technicians and veterinarians
Children in rodent-infested urban housing, particularly in lower-income areas
Immunocompromised individuals exposed to any rodents
People consuming water or food potentially contaminated by rat excreta (Haverhill fever outbreaks)
diagnosisOverview: A careful exposure history — specifically any recent rat contact, bite, scratch, or consumption of potentially rat-contaminated food or water — is the foundation of diagnosis, because streptobacillosis mimics many other febrile illnesses including viral infections, reactive arthritis, rheumatic fever, and leptospirosis. Blood cultures using enriched media (blood or serum-supplemented agar, or a BACTEC bottle incubated for up to 14 days) are the definitive diagnostic test; S. moniliformis grows slowly and standard protocols that terminate incubation at 5–7 days will miss it, so the laboratory must be specifically informed of the suspected diagnosis. Joint aspirate, if effusion is present, can also yield the organism on culture. PCR targeting the 16S rRNA gene of S. moniliformis is increasingly available at reference laboratories and is more sensitive than culture, particularly when antibiotics have already been started. Serology using agglutination or immunofluorescence assays has been used in research settings but lacks standardisation for clinical use. The complete blood count shows leucocytosis and sometimes anaemia with repeated illness; inflammatory markers such as CRP and erythrocyte sedimentation rate are elevated. A false-positive VDRL test may occur, as in spirillosis. In patients with arthritis, synovial fluid analysis should be performed to exclude septic arthritis from other organisms. Echocardiography is indicated when endocarditis is clinically suspected based on fever, new murmur, or failure to improve with standard antibiotic doses.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Definitive diagnosis by culturing Streptobacillus moniliformis from blood; requires enriched media and extended incubation up to 14 days because the organism grows slowly.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Two to three sets of blood drawn from different sites at the time of fever; the laboratory must be told to use enriched media and extend incubation. Results may take several days to two weeks; empirical antibiotics are typically started while awaiting culture.

diagnosticTests.purpose: The most sensitive molecular test, detecting organism-specific genetic material in blood or joint fluid even when cultures are negative or antibiotics have been started before sampling.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A standard blood draw or joint aspirate is sent to a reference microbiology laboratory; results typically take 24–72 hours. Currently available mainly at academic and specialist centres.

diagnosticTests.purpose: When arthritis is present, aspirating and culturing joint fluid can confirm S. moniliformis as the causative agent and exclude other causes of septic arthritis.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A physician inserts a needle into the swollen joint under sterile conditions; the procedure takes 5–15 minutes and the fluid is sent for culture, cell count, and crystal analysis. Culture results take several days.

diagnosticTests.test: Complete blood count and inflammatory markers
diagnosticTests.purpose: Documents leucocytosis, elevated CRP and ESR consistent with systemic bacterial infection, and checks for anaemia or thrombocytopenia in more severe cases.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Standard blood draw; results available within a few hours at most laboratories.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Identifies vegetations on heart valves when endocarditis is suspected, and guides the duration and intensity of intravenous antibiotic therapy.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A painless ultrasound probe is placed on the chest; transthoracic echo takes 20–30 minutes. If results are equivocal, transoesophageal echocardiography provides higher resolution and requires a short sedation procedure.

diagnosticTests.purpose: False-positive syphilis serology occurs in a subset of rat-bite fever cases and can serve as a diagnostic clue; positive results must be followed up with specific treponemal testing to exclude true syphilis.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Routine blood draw with results available within hours.

treatmentOverview: Penicillin G remains the first-line treatment for streptobacillosis, with a clinical track record spanning over 80 years. For uncomplicated bacteraemia without endocarditis, intravenous or intramuscular penicillin G at 1.2 million units daily for 10–14 days, or high-dose oral amoxicillin for stable patients, is standard. Duration is critically important: courses shorter than 10 days are associated with higher relapse rates because S. moniliformis forms antibiotic-tolerant L-forms that can repopulate once bactericidal pressure is removed. Doxycycline 100 mg twice daily for 14 days is the established alternative for penicillin-allergic patients. Chloramphenicol and clindamycin have been used in cases with additional antimicrobial considerations. For endocarditis, management follows bacterial endocarditis guidelines: high-dose intravenous penicillin G (12–18 million units per day) plus gentamicin for 2 weeks, continued as penicillin monotherapy for a total of 4–6 weeks. S. moniliformis is naturally beta-lactamase-negative, so resistance to penicillin has not been a clinical problem to date, and fluoroquinolones have variable activity and are not recommended as primary therapy. Arthritis typically resolves with effective antibiotic treatment without requiring intra-articular drainage, though large effusions may warrant aspiration for diagnostic or symptomatic relief. Supportive care includes adequate hydration, antipyretics for fever management, and non-steroidal anti-inflammatory drugs for arthritis pain once infection is under control.

medicalTreatments.name: Penicillin G (intravenous or intramuscular)
medicalTreatments.description: First-line therapy for all forms of streptobacillosis; 1.2 million units daily for 10–14 days for uncomplicated disease. High-dose IV penicillin G at 12–18 million units per day in divided doses is used for endocarditis. The organism has never demonstrated clinically significant penicillin resistance.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Clinical cure in over 95% of uncomplicated cases completing a full 10–14 day course; historical mortality reduced from approximately 10% untreated to under 2% with treatment.

medicalTreatments.name: Amoxicillin (oral)
medicalTreatments.description: An oral penicillin-class antibiotic used for uncomplicated streptobacillosis in stable, ambulatory patients; typically 500 mg three times daily for 10–14 days. Suitable for step-down therapy after initial intravenous treatment.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Equivalent clinical cure rates to parenteral penicillin for uncomplicated infections, approximately 90–95% with a complete course.

medicalTreatments.name: Doxycycline (oral)
medicalTreatments.description: The standard second-line agent for penicillin-allergic patients; 100 mg twice daily for 14 days. Active against Streptobacillus moniliformis in vitro and in clinical experience. Not recommended in pregnancy or children under 8 years.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Clinical cure expected in approximately 85–90% of penicillin-allergic patients completing the full course.

medicalTreatments.name: Penicillin G plus gentamicin (endocarditis protocol)
medicalTreatments.description: For Streptobacillus endocarditis: IV penicillin G 12–18 million units per day combined with gentamicin 3 mg/kg/day in divided doses for the first 2 weeks, followed by penicillin monotherapy to complete 4–6 weeks total, following standard infective endocarditis guidelines.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Survival in streptobacillosis endocarditis reported at approximately 80–90% with timely diagnosis and guideline-adherent therapy.

medicalTreatments.name: Chloramphenicol
medicalTreatments.description: Reserved for severe cases where both penicillin allergy and tetracyclines are contraindicated (for example, in young children with penicillin allergy); limited modern clinical data but established historical activity against S. moniliformis.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Clinical cure reported in case series, with cure rates approximately 80–85%; serious bone marrow toxicity risk limits routine use.

alternativeTreatments.name: NSAIDs for arthritis pain management
alternativeTreatments.description: Non-steroidal anti-inflammatory drugs such as ibuprofen can reduce joint swelling and pain during the arthritic phase once the diagnosis is confirmed and antibiotics are started; they are symptom adjuncts, not treatments for the infection.

alternativeTreatments.name: Wound care at time of bite
alternativeTreatments.description: Immediate thorough washing with soap and water, antiseptic irrigation, and tetanus prophylaxis at the time of any rat bite reduces bacterial inoculum and prevents secondary wound infection, even before systemic antibiotic prophylaxis is considered.

whySeeSpecialist: Streptobacillosis should be managed with infectious disease guidance when the diagnosis is suspected, because correct specimen collection and laboratory communication are essential for culture success, and because the differential diagnosis — particularly rheumatic fever, reactive arthritis, viral arthritis, and leptospirosis — requires systematic exclusion. A specialist is essential when endocarditis is a possibility, when the patient is immunocompromised, or when symptoms do not resolve within 48–72 hours of starting penicillin.
doctorSelectionGuide: Seek an infectious disease physician with experience in zoonotic infections and bacterial endocarditis. For cases with significant arthritis, co-management with a rheumatologist helps exclude rheumatic and reactive joint disorders. When endocarditis is suspected, cardiology input and access to echocardiography are required. In paediatric cases, a paediatric infectious disease specialist is ideal. Local or regional reference microbiology laboratories can provide PCR testing when standard blood cultures are negative.
preventionStrategies: Wear disposable gloves when handling rats — pet, laboratory, or wild — and wash hands with soap and water immediately after all rodent contact.
Seek medical assessment within 24 hours of any rat bite; ask whether a prophylactic antibiotic course (typically penicillin or amoxicillin for 3–5 days) is appropriate.
Teach children safe handling of pet rats, and supervise rat interactions to prevent playful bites reaching the skin.
Avoid consuming unpasteurised milk or water from sources that may be rodent-contaminated.
Rodent-proof homes and workplaces by sealing gaps, managing food waste, and implementing pest control measures.
Ensure tetanus immunisation is up to date before any high-risk rodent contact.
lifestyleModifications: Report any rat bite, however minor, to a healthcare provider; never self-manage and observe without consultation.
Keep pet rat environments clean, change bedding regularly, and minimise the animal's stress to reduce potential shedding.
Do not allow children to let pet rats explore their face or bite fingers; provide appropriate enrichment toys instead.
During active illness, rest, maintain fluid intake, and complete the full antibiotic course even after symptoms resolve.
Monitor joint symptoms carefully and return to the clinic promptly if a new joint becomes swollen or painful, as it may represent migrating infection or a developing complication.

recommended: Adequate oral fluids — at least 2 litres daily — to maintain hydration during febrile episodes
Protein-rich foods to support immune function and muscle recovery during prolonged illness
Calcium-rich foods and vitamin D during recovery if weight-bearing is impaired by arthritis
avoid: Alcohol, which worsens dehydration and can interact with doxycycline
Dairy products within two hours of doxycycline doses, as calcium impairs absorption
Unpasteurised milk or dairy products, which may carry the infecting organism in Haverhill fever contexts

exerciseGuidelines: Rest is required during the febrile and acute arthritic phase. Weight-bearing on affected joints should be minimised until swelling and pain resolve, typically within 1–2 weeks of starting antibiotics. Gentle range-of-motion exercises can begin once joint symptoms have substantially improved. Full return to physical activity should be gradual over 2–4 weeks; patients who develop endocarditis require cardiology clearance before resuming strenuous exercise.
dailyManagement: Complete the full 10–14 day antibiotic course without interruption — early stopping is the main cause of relapse.
Record which joints are affected and when, to document the migratory arthritis pattern and monitor resolution.
Return to the clinic if fever persists beyond 72 hours of starting antibiotics, new joints become involved, or symptoms worsen.
Avoid vigorous weight-bearing or exercise involving affected joints until swelling has fully resolved.
For Haverhill fever cluster cases, notify public health authorities to identify the contaminated food or water source and prevent further cases.
prognosis: With prompt diagnosis and a full course of penicillin or doxycycline, streptobacillosis resolves completely in the large majority of patients. Fever and systemic symptoms typically improve within 24–72 hours of starting effective antibiotics, and joint symptoms resolve over 1–2 weeks. Untreated streptobacillosis carries an estimated mortality of approximately 10%, with most deaths attributable to endocarditis. In the antibiotic era, mortality from uncomplicated cases is below 2%. Haverhill fever has a similar prognosis. Persistent arthritis after completing antibiotics is uncommon but has been reported, requiring rheumatological assessment to distinguish post-infectious arthritis from ongoing infection. Immunocompromised patients warrant closer monitoring for prolonged or recurrent disease.
recoveryTimeline: Fever and systemic symptoms improve within 24–72 hours of initiating effective antibiotics in most patients. Arthritis typically subsides over 7–14 days as the infection is cleared; joint effusions may persist a few days longer. Fatigue and malaise can continue for 1–2 weeks beyond fever resolution. The full 10–14 day antibiotic course must be completed. Endocarditis cases require 4–6 weeks of intravenous treatment and 4–6 weeks of convalescence before return to full activity. Any fever recurrence after completing treatment should prompt same-day reassessment.
complications: Infective endocarditis — the most life-threatening complication, occurring in a small percentage of untreated or inadequately treated cases; requires 4–6 weeks of high-dose intravenous penicillin and carries a mortality risk of 10–20% even with treatment.
Septic arthritis — while most arthritic episodes resolve with antibiotics, S. moniliformis can occasionally cause true purulent joint infection requiring surgical drainage.
Meningitis — rare central nervous system involvement with severe headache, neck stiffness, and photophobia; requires high-dose antibiotics with CNS penetration.
Myocarditis and pericarditis — cardiac muscle and pericardial inflammation causing arrhythmias, chest pain, and reduced cardiac function.
Amnionitis and foetal loss — streptobacillosis during pregnancy is associated with premature labour and foetal death in reported cases.
Relapse after inadequate treatment — failure to complete the full antibiotic course is the most common preventable cause of recurrent bacteraemia and arthritis.

supportResources.name: CDC — Rat-Bite Fever
supportResources.description: Comprehensive patient and clinician overview of rat-bite fever including streptobacillosis, prevention, and treatment from the U.S. Centers for Disease Control and Prevention.

supportResources.name: NIH StatPearls — Rat-Bite Fever
supportResources.description: Evidence-based clinical review of streptobacillosis and spirillosis covering pathogenesis, diagnosis, and antibiotic management.

supportResources.name: NHS — Animal and human bites
supportResources.description: UK NHS guidance on managing animal bites including when to seek medical care and antibiotic prophylaxis considerations.

sources.title: Elliott SP. Rat bite fever and Streptobacillus moniliformis. Clinical Microbiology Reviews, 2007
Graves MH, Janda JM. Rat-bite fever (Streptobacillus moniliformis): a potential emerging disease. International Journal of Infectious Diseases, 2001

confusedWithConditions.name: Spirillosis
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Spirillosis is caused by Spirillum minus and is characterised by wound re-ulceration at the bite site and a relapsing fever pattern; arthritis is uncommon. Streptobacillosis causes prominent migratory polyarthritis, normal wound healing, and sustained fever.

confusedWithConditions.name: Leptospirosis
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Leptospirosis is acquired from water or soil contaminated with rodent urine — not a bite wound — and characteristically causes severe myalgia, conjunctival injection, and jaundice or kidney injury; it is diagnosed by Leptospira serology.

confusedWithConditions.name: Dengue fever
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Dengue is a mosquito-transmitted arboviral disease causing severe joint and bone pain, retro-orbital pain, and thrombocytopenia; it does not follow rat bites and is diagnosed by NS1 antigen or serology.

confusedWithConditions.name: Louse-borne relapsing fever
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Relapsing fever from Borrelia recurrentis is transmitted by body lice, not rat bites; it causes a distinctly relapsing fever pattern, and the diagnosis is made by blood smear showing Borrelia spirochaetes.

relatedConditions.name: Spirillosis
relatedConditions.relevance: The other form of rat-bite fever, caused by Spirillum minus and predominating in Asia; distinguishing between the two is clinically important because spirillosis shows wound re-ulceration and relapsing fever while streptobacillosis shows migratory arthritis.

relatedConditions.name: Leptospirosis
relatedConditions.relevance: Another rodent-associated zoonosis that presents with fever and systemic illness and must be distinguished from streptobacillosis; both respond to penicillin or doxycycline but require different diagnostic workups.

relatedConditions.name: Cat-scratch disease
relatedConditions.relevance: A zoonotic bacterial infection transmitted through cat bites and scratches causing lymphadenopathy and fever, another important differential in patients with animal bites and febrile illness.

relatedConditions.name: Tularemia
relatedConditions.relevance: A bacterial zoonosis transmitted through animal bites, ticks, or contact with infected animal tissues that can mimic streptobacillosis with fever, lymphadenopathy, and systemic illness.

faqs.question: What is streptobacillosis?
faqs.answer: Streptobacillosis is a bacterial infection caused by Streptobacillus moniliformis, the most common form of rat-bite fever in North America and Europe. It causes fever, joint pain, and a skin rash after a rat bite or contact with rat-contaminated food or water.

faqs.question: How do you catch streptobacillosis?
faqs.answer: Most cases follow a rat bite or scratch. The bacteria colonise the mouths of 50–100% of apparently healthy rats. Less commonly, ingesting food or water contaminated by rat excreta causes an outbreak form called Haverhill fever.

faqs.question: How quickly does streptobacillosis develop after a rat bite?
faqs.answer: Symptoms usually start 3–10 days after the bite, though the incubation can range from 1 to 22 days. The bite wound typically heals normally before fever, joint pain, and rash appear.

faqs.question: Is streptobacillosis the same as rat-bite fever?
faqs.answer: Streptobacillosis is one of the two main forms of rat-bite fever. It predominates in North America and Europe. The other form, spirillosis, is caused by Spirillum minus and is the main type in Asia. Both are treated with penicillin.

faqs.question: What are the main symptoms of streptobacillosis?
faqs.answer: The typical symptoms are abrupt fever, migratory joint pain affecting knees, ankles, elbows, or wrists in up to 70% of patients, and a maculopapular or petechial rash on the trunk, hands, and feet that appears 2–4 days after fever starts.

faqs.question: Can streptobacillosis be cured?
faqs.answer: Yes. A 10–14 day course of penicillin cures over 95% of cases. Doxycycline is used for penicillin-allergic patients. Completing the full course is essential because stopping early is the main cause of relapse.

faqs.question: Is streptobacillosis dangerous?
faqs.answer: Untreated streptobacillosis carries about a 10% mortality rate, mainly from endocarditis. With appropriate penicillin treatment, mortality drops below 2% and most patients recover fully.

faqs.question: Can standard blood cultures detect the bacteria?
faqs.answer: Standard blood culture protocols often miss S. moniliformis because the organism grows slowly and requires enriched media. The laboratory must be told to use serum-supplemented culture and extend incubation to 14 days; PCR from blood is more sensitive when available.

faqs.question: Does streptobacillosis cause joint pain?
faqs.answer: Yes. Migratory polyarthritis is one of the hallmark features, occurring in 50–70% of patients. Multiple large joints become swollen and painful in turn over days. This distinguishes streptobacillosis from spirillosis, where arthritis is uncommon.

faqs.question: What is Haverhill fever?
faqs.answer: Haverhill fever is the name for streptobacillosis acquired through eating or drinking rat-contaminated food or water rather than a bite. It can affect multiple people from a shared source and typically causes more nausea and vomiting than bite-acquired cases.

faqs.question: How is streptobacillosis different from spirillosis?
faqs.answer: Streptobacillosis causes prominent joint pain, normal wound healing, and sustained fever; it predominates in North America. Spirillosis causes wound re-ulceration at the original bite site and relapsing fever; it predominates in Asia. Both are treated with penicillin.

faqs.question: What antibiotic is used for streptobacillosis?
faqs.answer: Penicillin G or oral amoxicillin for 10–14 days is the first-line treatment. Doxycycline is the standard alternative for penicillin-allergic patients. Endocarditis requires 4–6 weeks of high-dose intravenous penicillin with gentamicin for the first 2 weeks.

faqs.question: Can you get streptobacillosis from a pet rat?
faqs.answer: Yes. Pet rats from shops, breeders, and rescues can carry S. moniliformis without any signs of illness. Children with pet rats are the most commonly affected group in recent U.S. case reports. Gloves and prompt wound care after any bite reduce risk.

faqs.question: What should I do immediately after a rat bite?
faqs.answer: Wash the wound thoroughly with soap and water for at least 5 minutes, apply antiseptic, and see a doctor within 24 hours. A short prophylactic antibiotic course and a tetanus immunity check may be offered depending on your vaccination history and bite circumstances.

faqs.question: Is streptobacillosis reportable to health authorities?
faqs.answer: Rat-bite fever is a reportable disease in many U.S. states. Reporting allows public health authorities to track exposure sources, particularly pet shop chains or communal water supplies, and prevent further cases.

faqs.question: What complications can streptobacillosis cause?
faqs.answer: The most serious complication is bacterial endocarditis, which accounts for most deaths. Other complications include septic arthritis, meningitis, myocarditis, and in pregnancy, miscarriage or preterm labour. Complications occur mainly when treatment is delayed or incomplete.

faqs.question: How long does recovery from streptobacillosis take?
faqs.answer: Fever improves within 24–72 hours of starting antibiotics. Joint symptoms typically resolve in 7–14 days. Fatigue may persist 1–2 weeks after fever resolves. The full 10–14 day antibiotic course must be completed even after symptoms improve.

faqs.question: Can streptobacillosis come back after treatment?
faqs.answer: Relapse is uncommon after a correctly completed 10–14 day antibiotic course. It occurs most often when treatment is stopped early. Any recurrence of fever or joint symptoms after completing antibiotics warrants same-day medical review.

faqs.question: Why does streptobacillosis cause a rash?
faqs.answer: The rash results from immune activation and, in some patients, bacterial emboli in the skin during bacteraemia. The characteristic maculopapular or petechial rash appears on the trunk and extremities, particularly the palms and soles, 2–4 days after fever onset.

faqs.question: Do all rats carry the bacteria that causes streptobacillosis?
faqs.answer: Studies show 50–100% of wild and domestic rats carry S. moniliformis in their nasopharynx without appearing ill. There is no reliable way to identify a carrier animal by appearance; all rat bites should be treated as potentially infectious.

regionalNames.name: Haverhill fever
regionalNames.region: United Kingdom / United States (oral transmission form)

metaDescription: Streptobacillosis is a rat-bite fever with fever, migratory joint pain and rash. Learn causes, diagnosis and penicillin treatment. Doctor-reviewed guide.
